root_text.an8.13:1.1: “Aṭṭhahi, bhikkhave, aṅgehi samannāgato rañño bhaddo assājānīyo rājāraho hoti rājabhoggo, rañño aṅganteva saṅkhaṁ gacchati.
root_text.an8.13:1.3: Idha, bhikkhave, rañño bhaddo assājānīyo ubhato sujāto hoti—
root_text.an8.13:1.5: Yassaṁ disāyaṁ aññepi bhaddā assājānīyā jāyanti, tassaṁ disāyaṁ jāto hoti.
root_text.an8.13:1.9: Jegucchī hoti uccāraṁ vā passāvaṁ vā abhinisīdituṁ vā abhinipajjituṁ vā.
root_text.an8.13:1.10: Sorato hoti sukhasaṁvāso, na ca aññe asse ubbejetā.
root_text.an8.13:1.11: Yāni kho panassa honti sāṭheyyāni kūṭeyyāni jimheyyāni vaṅkeyyāni, tāni yathābhūtaṁ sārathissa āvikattā hoti.
root_text.an8.13:1.14: ‘Kāmaññe assā vahantu vā mā vā, ahamettha vahissāmī’ti cittaṁ uppādeti.
root_text.an8.13:1.16: Thāmavā hoti yāva jīvitamaraṇapariyādānā thāmaṁ upadaṁsetā.
root_text.an8.13:1.17: Imehi kho, bhikkhave, aṭṭhahi aṅgehi samannāgato rañño bhaddo assājānīyo rājāraho hoti rājabhoggo, rañño aṅganteva saṅkhaṁ gacchati.
root_text.an8.13:2.1: Evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, aṭṭhahi dhammehi samannāgato bhikkhu āhuneyyo hoti …pe… anuttaraṁ puññakkhettaṁ lokassa.
root_text.an8.13:2.3: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sīlavā hoti, pātimokkhasaṁvarasaṁvuto viharati ācāragocarasampanno aṇumattesu vajjesu bhayadassāvī, samādāya sikkhati sikkhāpadesu.
root_text.an8.13:2.8: jegucchī hoti anekavihitānaṁ pāpakānaṁ akusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ samāpattiyā.
root_text.an8.13:2.9: Sorato hoti sukhasaṁvāso, na aññe bhikkhū ubbejetā.
root_text.an8.13:2.10: Yāni kho panassa honti sāṭheyyāni kūṭeyyāni jimheyyāni vaṅkeyyāni, tāni yathābhūtaṁ āvikattā hoti satthari vā viññūsu vā sabrahmacārīsu.
root_text.an8.13:2.11: Tesamassa satthā vā viññū vā sabrahmacārī abhinimmadanāya vāyamati.
root_text.an8.13:2.13: ‘Kāmaññe bhikkhū sikkhantu vā mā vā, ahamettha sikkhissāmī’ti cittaṁ uppādeti.
root_text.an8.13:2.18: ‘kāmaṁ taco ca nhāru ca aṭṭhi ca avasissatu, sarīre upassussatu maṁsalohitaṁ; yaṁ taṁ purisathāmena purisavīriyena purisaparakkamena pattabbaṁ, na taṁ apāpuṇitvā vīriyassa saṇṭhānaṁ bhavissatī’ti.
root_text.an8.13:2.19: Imehi kho, bhikkhave, aṭṭhahi dhammehi samannāgato bhikkhu āhuneyyo hoti …pe… anuttaraṁ puññakkhettaṁ lokassā”ti.

translation_text.an8.13:1.1: “Mendicants, a fine royal thoroughbred with eight factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and reckoned a factor of kingship.
translation_text.an8.13:1.3: It’s when a fine royal thoroughbred is well born on both
translation_text.an8.13:1.4: the mother’s and the father’s sides.
translation_text.an8.13:1.5: He’s bred in the region fine thoroughbreds come from.
translation_text.an8.13:1.8: he eats carefully, without making a mess.
translation_text.an8.13:1.9: He’s disgusted by sitting or lying down in excrement or urine.
translation_text.an8.13:1.10: He’s gentle and pleasant to live with, and he doesn’t upset the other horses.
translation_text.an8.13:1.11: He openly shows his tricks, bluffs, ruses, and feints to his trainer,
translation_text.an8.13:1.12: so the trainer can try to subdue them.
translation_text.an8.13:1.14: ‘Whether or not the other horses carry their loads, I’ll carry mine.’
translation_text.an8.13:1.15: He always walks in a straight path.
translation_text.an8.13:1.16: He’s strong, and stays strong even until death.
translation_text.an8.13:1.17: A fine royal thoroughbred with these eight factors is worthy of a king. …
translation_text.an8.13:2.1: In the same way, a mendicant with eight qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with joined palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.
translation_text.an8.13:2.3: It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken.
translation_text.an8.13:2.7: They’re disgusted with bad conduct by way of body, speech, or mind,
translation_text.an8.13:2.8: and by attainment of the many kinds of unskillful things.
translation_text.an8.13:2.9: They're gentle and pleasant to live with, and they don’t upset the other mendicants.
translation_text.an8.13:2.10: They openly show their tricks, bluffs, ruses, and feints to their sensible spiritual companions,
translation_text.an8.13:2.11: so they can try to subdue them.
translation_text.an8.13:2.13: ‘Whether or not the other mendicants do their training, I’ll do mine.’
translation_text.an8.13:2.14: They always walk in a straight path.
translation_text.an8.13:2.15: And here the straight path is
translation_text.an8.13:2.16: right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
translation_text.an8.13:2.18: ‘Gladly, let my skin, sinews, and bones remain! Let the blood and flesh waste away in my body! I will not stop trying until I have achieved what is possible by human strength, energy, and vigor.’
translation_text.an8.13:2.19: A mendicant with these eight qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with joined palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.”

variant_text.an8.13:1.11: kho panassa honti → yāni kho panassa tāni (sya-all)
